1: gnōrizomen de hymin adelphoi tēn charin tou theou tēn dedomenēn en tais ekklēsiais tēs Makedonias: 'We want you to know, brothers, about the grace of God that has been given among the churches of Macedonia.' Charis (grace) is applied to the Macedonians' generous giving — the entire collection discourse of 2 Corinthians 8-9 is framed as charis-activity, emphasizing that giving flows from grace received and given. Gnōrizō (make known, bring to knowledge) — Paul is presenting the Macedonian example as an exemplum for the Corinthians to imitate, a standard Greco-Roman rhetorical appeal to a distinguished precedent.
2: hoti en pollē dokimē thlipseōs hē perissia tēs charas autōn kai hē kata bathous ptōcheia autōn eperisseusen eis ton ploutos tēs haplotētos autōn: 'that in a great ordeal of affliction, their abundance of joy and their extreme poverty have overflowed in a wealth of generosity.' Dokimē (tested quality, the result of testing) — the affliction has proven their character. Ptōcheia kata bathous (poverty according to depth, 'extreme poverty') — kata bathous (down to the depths) is an unusual intensifying phrase. Haplotēs (generosity/simplicity) — a key word in 2 Corinthians 8-9: it means both 'singleness/simplicity of mind' and 'generosity without calculation' (cf. 2 Corinthians 9:11, 13; Romans 12:8).
3: hoti kata dynamin martyrō kai hyper dynamin authairetoi: 'For they gave according to their ability, as I testify, and beyond their ability, of their own accord.' Kata dynamin ... hyper dynamin (according to ability ... beyond ability) — the prepositional pairs frame the super-natural generosity. Authairetos (of one's own choice, self-chosen, voluntarily) — from autos (self) + haireomai (choose); a NT hapax legomenon in this form. The Macedonians' giving was not solicited or coerced but freely chosen from within — the model of voluntary generosity that Paul will argue for in 2 Corinthians 9:5-7.
4: meta pollēs paraklēseōs deomenoi hēmōn tēn charin kai tēn koinōnian tēs diakonias tēs eis tous hagious: 'begging us earnestly for the favor of taking part in the relief of the saints.' Deomenoi (begging, pleading — present participle) — the Macedonians requested to participate in the collection, rather than being recruited. Koinōnia tēs diakonias (fellowship/partnership of the service) — the collection is simultaneously koinōnia (participatory fellowship) and diakonia (service/ministry); the two terms bracket the theological significance of giving as both relational solidarity and active service. Tous hagious (the saints/holy ones) — Paul's standard term for the Jerusalem community.
5: kai ou kathōs ēlpisamen all heautous edōkan prōton tō kyriō kai hēmin dia thelēmatos theou: 'and this, not as we expected, but they gave themselves first to the Lord and then by the will of God to us.' The climax of the Macedonian example: before the money, they gave heautous (themselves, reflexive pronoun). The prōton ... kai (first ... and then) order is crucial: prior self-surrender to the Lord makes the financial gift an expression of an already-committed life rather than a transaction. Tō kyriō ... hēmin (to the Lord ... to us) — the donation is both a vertical act (to the Lord) and a horizontal act (through the apostolic channel Paul represents).
6: eis to parakalasai hēmas Titon hina kathōs proenerato houtōs kai epitelesei eis hymas kai tēn charin tautēn: 'so that we urged Titus that as he had started, so he should also complete among you this act of grace.' Proenerato (had begun beforehand) — the pro- prefix marks prior beginning; Titus had previously started the collection initiative with the Corinthians. Epiteleō (bring to completion, complete) — used of completing a religious duty or vow in Hellenistic Greek. The collection is consistently called charis (grace) — giving is a graced activity, not merely a philanthropic transaction.
7: all hōsper en panti perisseuete pistei kai logō kai gnōsei kai pasē spoudē kai tē ex hēmōn en hymin agapē: 'But as you excel in everything — in faith, in speech, in knowledge, in all earnestness, and in our love for you — see that you excel in this act of grace also.' Perisseuete (abound/excel/overflow) — the Corinthians' giftedness; this catalog (faith, speech, knowledge, diligence, love) echoes the gift-list of 1 Corinthians 1:5 and 12-14. The ironic edge: the community famous for its spiritual gifts is now invited to add generosity to the list. Ina kai en tautē tē chariti perisseuēte — the hortatory hina: excel in this grace also.
8: ou kat epitagēn legō alla dia tēs heterōn spoudēs kai to tēs hymeteras agapēs gnēsion dokimazōn: 'I say this not as a command, but to prove by the eagerness of others the genuineness of your love.' Epitagē (command, authoritative order) — Paul refuses the command-frame for financial giving; the collection must come from genuine freedom, not apostolic coercion. Gnēsion (genuineness, legitimacy) from gnēsios (legitimate-born, genuine): the genunine versus counterfeit contrast. Dokimazō (test, prove, discern) — Paul is testing/proving the quality of their love through comparison with Macedonia's example. The test reveals what is already there, not creates something new.
9: gnōsete gar tēn charin tou kyriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou hoti di hymas eptōcheusen plousios ōn: 'For you know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor.' Eptōcheusen (became poor, aorist — the historical moment of incarnation): from ptōchos (utterly destitute, beggar). Plousios ōn (though he was rich) — present participle of condition simultaneous with the aorist main verb: while being rich, he became poor. The theological claim: the eternal Son's pre-incarnate state is described as ploutos (wealth/richness), his incarnation as ptōcheia (destitution). Hina hymeis tē ekeinēs ptōcheia ploutēsēte: 'so that by his poverty you might become rich' — the soteriological exchange through incarnational condescension.
10: kai gnōmēn en toutō didōmi touto gar hymin sympherei: 'And in this matter I give my judgment: for this benefits you.' Gnōmē (judgment/opinion/advice) — distinguished from epitagē (command, 2 Corinthians 8:8); Paul gives counsel, not an apostolic directive. The same distinction appears in 1 Corinthians 7:6, 25 (advice vs. command about marriage). Sympherei (benefits/is expedient for) — the practical benefit of completing what they began. Hoi proēnēxanto proteron kai to thelein proenerasthente apo perusi: 'who started a year ago not only to do this work but also to desire to do it.' The thelein (willing) preceded the poiein (doing) by a year — intention without execution.
11: nuni de kai to poiēsai epitolesate hopōs kathaper hē prothumia tou thelein houtōs kai to epitolesai ek tou echein: 'So now finish doing it as well, so that your readiness in desiring it may be matched by your completing it out of what you have.' Epiteleō (bring to completion) — the command (epitolesate, aorist imperative) to finish what was begun. Prothumia (eagerness/readiness/willing spirit) — a key term in 2 Corinthians 8:11, 12, 19 and 2 Corinthians 9:2: the inner disposition that should match the outer performance. Ek tou echein (out of what you have) — giving from one's actual means, not hypothetical abundance.
12: ei gar hē prothumia prokeitai euprosdektos katho ean echē ou katho ouk echei: 'For if the readiness is there, it is acceptable according to what a person has, not according to what he does not have.' Prokeitai (lie before, be present/ready) — the eagerness as already there, pre-existing the gift. Euprosdektos (fully acceptable, well-received) — the compound with eu- (well) and pros- (toward) intensifies acceptability. The principle stated here — giving according to means, not beyond means — qualifies the Macedonian example of 2 Corinthians 8:3 (hyper dynamin): the principle is proportionality, not a requirement of extraordinary sacrifice beyond one's actual capacity.
13: ou gar hina allois anesis hymin thlipsis all ex isotētos: 'For I do not mean that others should be eased and you burdened, but that as a matter of equality.' Anesis (ease/relief) vs. thlipsis (burden/pressure) — the economic asymmetry Paul is trying to address. Isotēs (equality/fairness) — the governing principle: not that everyone must give identically, but that the distribution aims at equality across the network. Isotēs in Greek political philosophy was a key democratic virtue, used also in contracts and commercial law for fair dealing; Paul uses it for the economic ethics of the new covenant community.
14: en tō nyn kairō to hymōn perisseuma eis to ekeinōn hysterēma hina kai to ekeinōn perisseuma genētai eis to hymōn hysterēma: 'your abundance at the present time should supply their need, so that their abundance may supply your need.' Perisseuma (abundance/surplus) and hysterēma (deficiency/lack/need) — the two contrasting states that the collection addresses. The principle of reciprocal sufficiency: the Corinthians' current economic abundance addresses Jerusalem's need; Jerusalem's spiritual abundance (from which the Gentiles received the gospel) addresses the Corinthians' spiritual poverty. Hopōs genētai isotēs (so that there may be equality) — the economic balance that reflects the body-of-Christ interdependence.
15: kathōs gegraptai ho to poly ouk epleonasen kai ho to oligon ouk ēlattónesen: Citation of Exodus 16:18 (the manna narrative). In the LXX, each Israelite gathered manna and measured it; those who gathered much had no surplus, those who gathered little had no shortage — the miraculous equalization of divine provision. Paul applies this typologically to the collection: the economic redistribution through giving replicates the manna-principle of divinely-ordered sufficiency. The word pleonazō (have more than enough, increase) will recur in 2 Corinthians 9:10 (God who multiplies the seed).
16: charis de tō theō tō donton tēn autēn spoudēn hyper hymōn en tē kardia Titou: 'But thanks be to God who put the same earnestness for you into the heart of Titus.' Charis tō theō (thanks to God) — a liturgical doxology used by Paul at key moments of gratitude (2 Corinthians 2:14; 2 Corinthians 9:15). Spoudē (earnestness/diligence) — the same word used of the Macedonians' eagerness; God creates the same disposition in Titus. Didōmi eis tēn kardian (give into the heart) — the same idiom as God 'putting' things into hearts (2 Corinthians 3:2-3; Hebrews 8:10 Jeremiah 31:33); divine agency in human motivation.
17: hoti tēn men paraklēsin edexato spoudaioteros de huparchōn authairetos exēlthen pros hymas: 'For he not only accepted our appeal, but being himself very earnest he is going to you of his own accord.' Authairetos (of his own accord, self-chosen) — repeating the word from 2 Corinthians 8:3 (Macedonians gave voluntarily); Titus's voluntary eagerness mirrors the Macedonians' voluntary generosity. Spoudaioteros (more earnest, comparative adjective) — the comparative marks Titus's response as exceeding Paul's appeal. The portrait of Titus as independently motivated (not simply Paul's agent) gives apostolic credibility to his visit.
18: synepempsamen de met autou ton adelphon hou ho epainos en tō euangeliō dia pasōn tōn ekklēsiōn: 'With him we are sending the brother who is famous among all the churches for his preaching of the gospel.' Synempsamen (we sent together, aorist) — the epistolary aorist, looking back from the letter's perspective at the sending that is current. The unnamed 'brother' whose fame in the gospel is mentioned has generated much speculation (Luke? Barnabas? Apollos?) — Paul's reticence here is likely intentional diplomatic discretion. Epainos (praise/fame/commendation) — the community-acknowledged reputation that commends this emissary.
19: ou monon de alla kai cheirotonytheisa hypo tōn ekklesiōn synekdemos hēmōn syn tē chariti tautē tē diakonoumenē: 'and not only that, but he has been appointed by the churches to travel with us as we carry out this act of grace.' Cheirotonētheis (elected/appointed by show of hands, or chosen) — from cheirotoneō (stretch out the hand), the Athenian democratic term for election by vote, then generalized to appointment/selection. The churches (not just Paul) have chosen this representative, emphasizing accountability and communal ownership of the collection. Synekdemos (fellow traveler, travel companion) — a compound of syn- (with) + ekdēmos (away from home).
20: stelloemnoi touto mē tis hēmas mōmēsētai en tē hadrothēti tautē tē diakonoumenē hyph hēmōn: 'We take this care so that no one should blame us in regard to this generous gift that is being administered by us.' Stellomai (take care, avoid, guard against) — used of careful management and self-restraint. Hadrothēs (abundance/munificence) — an unusual word (hapax in NT) from hadros (thick, large, full-grown); the gift is described by its physical abundance. Mōmēsētai (blame, find fault with, from mōmos, blame) — Paul is protecting the collection's integrity from any suspicion of financial impropriety. The accountability structures reflect real concerns about money-management in apostolic missions.
21: pronooumen gar kala ou monon enōpion kyriou alla kai enōpion anthrōpōn: 'for we aim at what is honorable not only in the Lord's sight but also in the sight of man.' A near-citation of Proverbs 3:4 LXX (pronoou kala enōpion kyriou kai anthrōpōn, 'provide good things before the Lord and before people'). Kala (honorable/beautiful/noble things) — the Greek ethical ideal of kalos (noble, beautiful, good) is invoked for the financial administration. Both vertical (before the Lord) and horizontal (before people) standards govern the collection's handling — a double accountability that refuses to collapse into either pure divine-only or social-approval motivation.
22: synepempsamen de autois ton adelphon hēmōn hon edokimasamen en pollois pollakis spoudaion onta: 'And with them we are sending our brother whom we have often tested and found earnest in many matters.' Dokimazō (test, prove, discern) — the same word used for testing the Corinthians' love (2 Corinthians 8:8); this brother has been repeatedly (pollakis) tested in many contexts (en pollois) and found genuinely spoudaios (earnest/diligent). The three-person delegation (Titus + the two brothers) provides maximal accountability: no single person handles the collection alone. Pepheidomenō de implies trust in the Corinthian situation has grown considerably.
23: eite hyper Titou koinōnos emos kai eis hymas synergos eite adelphoi hēmōn apostoloi ekklēsiōn doxa Christou: 'As for Titus, he is my partner and fellow worker for your benefit. And as for our brothers, they are messengers of the churches, the glory of Christ.' Koinōnos (partner, sharer) and synergos (co-worker, fellow worker) — Paul's relational vocabulary for apostolic collaboration. Apostoloi ekklēsiōn (envoys/messengers of the churches) — apostolos here in its non-technical sense: delegates sent on a specific mission. Doxa Christou (glory of Christ) — the delegation manifests Christ's glory in the collection's management; they are the embodiment of Christ's honor in the mission.
24: tēn oun endeixin tēs agapēs hymōn kai hēmōn kauchēseōs hyper hymōn eis autous endeiknymenoi eis prosōpon tōn ekklēsiōn: 'So give proof before the churches of your love and of our boasting about you to these men.' Endeixis (demonstration, proof, public evidence) — an endeixis is a public act that proves a claim, used in rhetorical and legal contexts. Eis prosōpon tōn ekklēsiōn (before the face of the churches) — the delegation represents all the churches, so the Corinthians' response will be observed by the whole network. The appeal mobilizes the Corinthians' honor-consciousness: their generosity will publicly vindicate Paul's boasting about them before all the partner-churches.
